response_raw:
{
    "mode": "human",
    "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
    "opinion": "Having spent many years running my own plumbing business, I've learned that tackling problems requires a methodical approach. Dealing with fake content on social media is no different; it's like when customers reported faulty pipes but couldn't identify the exact leak—without proper diagnosis and action, things can get messy fast.\n\nAs someone who enjoys photography and cycling, I appreciate being part of communities where people share genuine experiences. Seeing manipulated images or misleading information online feels similar to a customer showing me photoshopped evidence when we're trying to fix their real plumbing issues. You have to look for the inconsistencies: maybe an image looks too perfect, or a story doesn't add up logically.\n\nI believe that being agreeable and conscientious helps in handling this situation effectively—my master's degree also gives me tools to analyze data properly. We spend several hours daily on social media, so I've developed ways to stay vigilant without getting overwhelmed by the sheer volume of content.",
    "reasoning_summary": "First, identify fake content by cross-referencing with reliable sources and checking for inconsistencies like poor grammar or unrealistic claims.\nThen, verify information through multiple channels—just as we check pipe measurements against blueprints in my profession.\nFinally, decide whether to report it or simply avoid engagement if the source is untrustworthy.",
    "tone": "Practical yet concerned",

    "safety_notes": "None",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
Having spent many years running my own plumbing business, I've learned that tackling problems requires a methodical approach. Dealing with fake content on social media is no different; it's like when customers reported faulty pipes but couldn't identify the exact leak—without proper diagnosis and action, things can get messy fast.

As someone who enjoys photography and cycling, I appreciate being part of communities where people share genuine experiences. Seeing manipulated images or misleading information online feels similar to a customer showing me photoshopped evidence when we're trying to fix their real plumbing issues. You have to look for the inconsistencies: maybe an image looks too perfect, or a story doesn't add up logically.

I believe that being agreeable and conscientious helps in handling this situation effectively—my master's degree also gives me tools to analyze data properly. We spend several hours daily on social media, so I've developed ways to stay vigilant without getting overwhelmed by the sheer volume of content.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
First, identify fake content by cross-referencing with reliable sources and checking for inconsistencies like poor grammar or unrealistic claims.
Then, verify information through multiple channels—just as we check pipe measurements against blueprints in my profession.
Finally, decide whether to report it or simply avoid engagement if the source is untrustworthy.
